statement: The Icelandic króna has experienced three major crisis episodes since 2000. During the 2008 banking collapse, the króna lost approximately 50% of its value against the euro (from ~90 ISK/EUR in mid-2007 to ~185 ISK/EUR by early 2009), necessitating capital controls that lasted from 2008 to 2017. A second episode of sharp depreciation occurred in early 2020 at the onset of COVID-19, with the króna weakening from ~138 to ~162 ISK/EUR (~17% depreciation). A third period of weakness occurred in 2023–2024, with the króna trading above 150 ISK/EUR. These episodes illustrate the vulnerability of a small independent currency, but critics note that króna flexibility also aided recovery — Iceland's post-2008 rebound was faster than that of peripheral eurozone members who could not devalue.
source_name: Central Bank of Iceland — Exchange rate data; ECB — Statistical Data Warehouse
caveats: Exchange rate flexibility can be both an advantage (automatic stabiliser during asymmetric shocks) and a disadvantage (imported inflation, uncertainty for trade and investment). The optimal currency area literature suggests Iceland is too small for an independent currency, but the eurozone is arguably not an optimal currency area either.

related_entries.statement: Iceland's inflation has been persistently higher and more volatile than in eurozone countries. Between 2000 and 2024, Iceland's average annual CPI inflation was approximately 4.7%, compared to the ECB's target of ~2%. During the 2008 financial crisis, Icelandic inflation spiked to 12.7% (2008) and 12.0% (2009), driven by the króna's collapse. A second inflationary episode occurred in 2022–2023, with CPI reaching 8.3% and 8.7% respectively — higher than the eurozone's peak of 8.4% in 2022. Even in calm periods (2014–2019), Iceland's inflation averaged 2.3%, consistently above the ECB's target.

The Central Bank of Iceland has consistently maintained higher interest rates than the European Central Bank. The CBI's key rate has averaged approximately 6.5% over the 2000–2025 period, compared to the ECB's average of roughly 1.5%. As of early 2026, the CBI policy rate stands at approximately 8.5%, while the ECB deposit facility rate is around 3.0%. This differential of ~5.5 percentage points translates directly into higher mortgage rates, business loan costs, and government borrowing costs. Pro-EU advocates argue that euro adoption would save Icelandic households and businesses billions of krónur annually in reduced interest expenses.
